coredata.dc:title: Pentetic acid derived dual purpose fluorescent carbon dots for pH responsive drug delivery and antioxidant proficiency
coredata.dc:creator: Hussain, Afzal
Alajmi, Mohamed Fahad
Ganguly, S.
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom-doped surface-engineered carbon dots provide substantial potential, not only in their fluorescence sensing capabilities but also as nanocarriers for medicinal compounds and in exhibiting antioxidant behaviour. Here we describe a way to make naturally occurring carbon dots (PACDs) utilizing pentetic acid and gelatin in a one-step thermal coupling procedure. Antioxidant activity, biocompatibility, and colloidal stability were all highlighted by the PACDs. In order to assess the PACDs' drug transport capabilities, anticancer model drug molecules were also attached to their surface. Prolonged release patterns were seen in the drug-containing PACDs, indicating that they could be used for controlled drug delivery. Cumulative release data has been fitted into the Higuchi and Weibull models to understand the medication delivery process. The PACDs showed release characteristics that were sensitive to changes in pH; at acidic pH, the release profile could be more precisely controlled (∼28 %) than at alkaline pH (∼59 %). At PACDs concentrations <1 mg/mL, the radical activity is >80 % active; however this activity is greatly affected by the concentration. Because of their low toxicity to living human cell lines, PACDs are an attractive and versatile nano-vector for use in biomedical studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Colloidal stability
Antioxidant activity
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of pentetic acid-gelatine carbon dots (PACDs)
Assessment of antioxidant activity using DPPH radical
Assessment of antioxidant activity using ABTS radical cation
In vitro cytocompatibility assessment of the PACDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author thanks the generous support from the researchers supporting project number ( RSPD2024R980 ) King Saud University , Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the CDs synthesis and its purification steps for further usages.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of PACDs in aqueous solution. Inset: CDs in daylight and under UV exposure (b) PL spectra of PACDs at different excitations (c) Red shift of the emission peak with excitation wavelength (d) Influence of UV exposure time on the fluorescence intensity of the PACDs. (e) Long-term stability of PACDs (f) XPS full survey spectra of PACDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) PL intensity basement for 90 days of PACDs (b) PL intensity assessment in NaCl solution (aq.) at different concentration showing insignificant changes (c) absorbance of PACDs and Rhodamine B for 60 min. Light exposure (d) PL intensities of PACDs and Rhodamine B for 60 min. Light exposure.
The size stability of the PACDs in PBS and foetal bovine serum (FBS) demonstrates consistent sizes for up to 7 days.
Radical scavenging activities of the CDs against (a) DPPH radicals, (b) ABTS radical cations (c) Different methods enable CDs' radical scavenging activity.
Schematic of drug loading onto PACDs and its release approach. Inset showing the interactions (H-bonding) between the surface polar groups of PACDs and drug molecule.

(a) UV–vis spectra of pure drug (DOX), PACDs, and drug-PACDs conjugate (b) Cumulative drug release plot at different pH (c) cumulative drug release behaviour of PACDs at different physiological conditions (d) Weibul model fitting at different pH release behaviour (f) Higuchi model fitting at different pH release behaviour.
Cell proliferation behaviour of PACDs nanoparticles was observed for 24 and 72 h.
Different type of CDs for drug delivery applications.

row.entry: Bioimaging and screening of anticancer activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparative study of other prepared quantum dots regarding their quantum yields and applicability.

head.ce:title: Pentetic acid derived dual purpose fluorescent carbon dots for pH responsive drug delivery and antioxidant proficiency

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacognosy, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, PO Box 2457, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacognosy, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Waterloo, Ontario, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Waterloo, Ontario, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom-doped surface-engineered carbon dots provide substantial potential, not only in their fluorescence sensing capabilities but also as nanocarriers for medicinal compounds and in exhibiting antioxidant behaviour. Here we describe a way to make naturally occurring carbon dots (PACDs) utilizing pentetic acid and gelatin in a one-step thermal coupling procedure. Antioxidant activity, biocompatibility, and colloidal stability were all highlighted by the PACDs. In order to assess the PACDs' drug transport capabilities, anticancer model drug molecules were also attached to their surface. Prolonged release patterns were seen in the drug-containing PACDs, indicating that they could be used for controlled drug delivery. Cumulative release data has been fitted into the Higuchi and Weibull models to understand the medication delivery process. The PACDs showed release characteristics that were sensitive to changes in pH; at acidic pH, the release profile could be more precisely controlled (∼28 %) than at alkaline pH (∼59 %). At PACDs concentrations <1 mg/mL, the radical activity is >80 % active; however this activity is greatly affected by the concentration. Because of their low toxicity to living human cell lines, PACDs are an attractive and versatile nano-vector for use in biomedical studies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are small fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, typically <20 nm in size. They possess remarkable chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, and high water dispersity, making them very suitable for various biomedical applications [1]. Initially, these entities were accidentally found during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes. Nowadays, they can be readily acquired from various materials using different convenient methods, such as stepwise organic synthesis from smaller precursors, microwave-mediated synthesis, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, electrochemical exfoliation, liquid phase exfoliation, and more [1,2]. CDs can be categorised into three distinct subtypes: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots, and carbonised polymer dots [3–5]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbonised polymer dots (CPDs) typically have spherical structures and are usually produced by the processes of assembly, crosslinking, polymerization, and carbonisation using small molecules, polymers, or biomass materials [6–8]. CQDs exhibit graphite structures with several layers, whereas carbonised polymer dots feature core-shell designs that are easily distinguishable [9–12]. The typical diameter of carbon dots present in carbonised polymer dots is <10 nm. The cores are surrounded by shells composed of diverse functional groups or polymer chains. Due to the inherent structural configuration, the carbonised polymer dots exhibit a notable level of stability and a reduced occurrence of anoxia.

Pentetic acid (PA), or diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA), is a polyamino-polycarboxylic acid characterised by its intricate molecular composition, which consists of many amine and carboxylic acid groups [13]. One notable attribute of this chemical is its notable chelation capacity, which allows it to form long-lasting complexes with a diverse array of metal ions. The utilization of pentetic acid and its derivatives in chelation therapy within the field of medicine serves the purpose of removing detrimental heavy metals from the human body. Moreover, it plays a crucial role in the advancement of radiopharmaceuticals employed for diagnostic imaging within the realm of nuclear medicine. In addition to its medical uses, pentetic acid is utilised in environmental contexts to address soil and water pollution by assisting in the containment and elimination of toxic metals [14]. The adaptability of this substance encompasses a wide range of commercial items, such as cleaning agents and detergents. Nevertheless, it is crucial to exercise prudence and follow expert advice while contemplating the therapeutic applications of this substance, owing to its potential adverse reactions and the requirement for accurate dosage.

In order to enhance the pharmacological activity of antibiotics, Ardekani and colleagues developed metronidazole-conjugated CDs [15]. To date, the therapeutic efficacy of drug nanocarriers utilizing CDs, which possess a singular surface characteristic (either PEGylated, negatively charged, or positively charged), has been observed to influence their potential applications. The process of PEGylation, which entails the attachment of PEG molecules to drug transporters, can extend the duration of their presence in the bloodstream [16]. This is because PEGylation acts as a protective layer, preventing rapid elimination of the carriers by the immune system. Additionally, the negatively charged property of the carriers helps to resist protein adsorption caused by electrostatic repulsion [17,18]. Nevertheless, PEGylation can hinder the absorption of drug carriers by cancer cells. Additionally, negatively charged drug carriers face difficulty in entering cancer cells due to electrostatic repulsion with the negatively charged cell membrane, leading to a reduction in therapeutic effectiveness [19,20]. Numerous studies have been conducted to enhance the properties of biochemical substances that can selectively identify and target cancer cells. Fluorescent dyes have been widely employed as agents for labelling cells. However, a major challenge for both fluorescent dyes and nanoparticles is to obtain selectivity for targeting cells and compatibility with biological systems [21,22]. To achieve cell targetability, fluorescent markers have been altered by including recognition elements and ligands that can specifically attach to cell surface receptors [23]. The Table 1 presents a succinct summary of several categories of CDs and their utilization as carriers for model pharmaceuticals. The table provides a comprehensive compilation of the origin of CDs, encompassing a diverse array of natural sources such as carrot [24], bamboo leaf extract [25], persimmon [26], saffron [27], milk [28], mulberry leaves [29], chitosan derivatives [30], and chrysanthemum derivatives [31]. The table shows different CDs were applied as carriers for several model drugs, such as mitomycin, doxorubicin, gemcitabine, prilocane, lisinopril, curcumin, lycorine, and telmisartan. The application of these drug-loaded CDs is also specified in the table, with a primary emphasis on their anticancer capabilities. Additionally, certain CDs are utilised for anesthetics, hypertension treatment, and renal failure therapy [28].

Over the past few decades, there has been a substantial amount of focus spent on the development of an environmentally friendly approach for the production of luminous CDs by the utilization of naturally existing precursors [37]. Gelatin, which is derived from naturally occurring polymers, has been chosen as the primary precursor material for the production of CDs. Gelatin is a biodegradable protein that is generated by partially hydrolyzing collagen. It has emerged as a topic of great interest in a variety of industries, including the photography industry, the pharmaceutical industry, and the cosmetics industry. A high degree of water solubility, a thermo-reversible sol-gel transition, non-toxicity, outstanding mechanical strength, and elasticity while in a dry condition are some of the favourable qualities that this material possesses [38]. In addition, gelatine has a strong propensity to functionalize with other molecules, which can result in improved individualization of its properties. Therefore, in order to produce N-doped CDs, thermal coupling of gelatine and pentetic acid has been carried out. Non-cytotoxic and drug carrier properties of the CDs are being evaluated. The CDs also showed excellent antioxidant behaviour against DPPH and ABTS radicals.

Pentetic acid, gelatine and quinine sulphate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Germany. The water for using the whole experiments was purified through a Millipore system (Bedford, MA, USA). Other chemicals used here were of analytical grades and were used without further purification. DPPH, and ABTS were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All other chemicals used here are of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pentetic acid-gelatine carbon dots (PACDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped carbon dots were synthesised through the application of thermal coupling to a mixture of pentetic acid (PA) and gelatine. 32 mg of PA and 191 mg of gelatine were dissolved in 12 mL of water and then evaporate to dryness followed by charring in a borosilicate glass vial. Next, 15 mL of distilled water was poured into the vial and kept ∼10 min at 50 °C. After that, the glass vial was cooled to the room temperature and then subjected to centrifugation at a speed of 12,000 rpm for duration of 15 min in order to isolate the larger particles. The suspension was dialyzed against Milli-Q water using a semi-permeable membrane (cellulose ester membrane bag with an average molecular weight of 3500 Da). The PACDs were subsequently subjected to freeze drying under vacuum conditions, resulting in the formation of a brownish powdered substance with a yield of around 28.6 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of antioxidant activity using DPPH radical
ce:section.ce:para: DPPH was used to assess the free radical scavenging capabilities of various concentrations of PACDs. The details have been given in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of antioxidant activity using ABTS radical cation
ce:section.ce:para: The ABTS radical cation decolorization test was used to determine the free radical scavenging activity [39]. The details have been given in supporting information.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX), a drug that dissolves well in water, was combined with an aqueous solution of PACDs (15 mg/100 mL; w/V). Subsequently, the system was agitated in a 100 mL beaker for an extended period of time, specifically overnight, lasting at least 10–14 h. While stirring, the mixture of the DOX and PACDs was sealed off sealed with a Parafilm stretch wrapper and the beaker was shielded from direct sunlight by covering it with aluminium foil. Subsequently, the mixture underwent centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 rpm for duration of 15 min to isolate any desired insoluble solid particles. Subsequently, the entire liquid portion containing the DOX-PACDs was congregated and put into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 10 kDa. It was then subjected to dialysis against MilliQ-water for duration of three consecutive days with occasional water change. In order to determine the amount of drug present, the liquid remaining after centrifugation was collected and measured using a calibration plot of DOX at a wavelength of 492 nm (λmax).

The drug loading efficiency was determined using the following equation:
(3)
Drug loading efficiency
%
=
Amount of
DOX / PACDs − amount of free
DOX
amount of
DOX
× 100

The drug release investigation was conducted using three distinct pH medium, specifically pH 5.6, 7.4, and 8.7. A total of ∼2 mg of drug were evenly distributed into 20 mL of PBS buffer solution, while maintaining the previously indicated pH. Subsequently, 10 mL of the dispersion was put into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100–200 Da and placed in a 500 mL glass beaker containing a PBS solution. The system was maintained at a consistent temperature of approximately 35-37 °C. While agitating the system, the entire beaker was shielded by an aluminium sheet. A 1.5 mL sample was collected at regular intervals and analysed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 492 nm. The diffusion of the DOX through the dialysis membrane was used as the control.

A one-step pyrolysis technique was used to synthesise gelatine and pentetic acid-based CDs (PACDs). The gelatine and pentetic acid were thermally coupled by means of carbonisation. The samples contained functional groups, which led to the production of amide groups, as determined by the surface characterizations. The entire process of synthesis is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The UV–visible spectra (Fig. 2a) of the CDs showed a peak at 270 nm, ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain of the CDs. The peak at 346 nm attributed to the n–π* transition of multi-conjugate CO of the CDs, respectively [40]. The inset revealed that the aqueous dispersion of PACDs appeared pale yellow in the daytime but exhibited a blue fluorescence under UV illumination. As depicted in Fig. 2b, when the excitation wavelength was changed from 320 to 420 nm, the emission spectra of the photoluminescent CDs, which had wavelengths ranging from 421 to 480 nm, displayed a variation in the intensity of the fluorescent emission signal. The red-shifted emission of PACDs at various excitation wavelengths is shown in Fig. 2c. The detected emission behaviour, which relies on excitation wavelength, fits with previous research [23,41]. This phenomenon occurs due to the presence of surface-emissive traps and the effects of the sizes of the CDs on the band gap. CDs possess several advantages compared to traditional organic dyes, including their limited susceptibility to photobleaching, chemical stability, and solubility in water. The photostability of the CDs was assessed by exposing them to continuous UV radiation for 180 min (Fig. 2d). The fluorescence signals emitted by PACDs were measured at different time intervals. The emission intensity of PACDs remained unchanged, as seen in Figure, even after 180 min of continuous UV irradiation. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains >98 % of their initial intensity even after being kept at 4 °C for 33 days (Fig. 2e). To preserve the CDs solution for longer durations, it can be dried in a vacuum oven to yield a powdered state. Subsequently, this powder can be resuspended in water before use. Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to examine the elemental composition and surface states of PACDs. The XPS spectra (Fig. 2f) displayed four distinct peaks at 284.26, 400.26, and 532.99, which corresponded to the presence of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively [42]. The presence of polar functionalities in PACDs increases their stability in polar solvents, which makes them highly beneficial for use as fluorescence probes. Table 2 discusses different types of CDs synthesis methods, optical properties and applications in comparison to this work. Our developed work distinguishes itself by utilizing pentetic acid and gelatine as precursors, as well as employing the pyrolysis process for synthesis, in addition to the methods and precursors mentioned. The provided sample yields CDs exhibiting a distinct luminescence wavelength range spanning from 320 to 420 nm, accompanied by blue photoluminescence. The CDs possess prospective uses in drug delivery and antioxidant characteristics, indicating their potential usefulness in biomedical contexts focused on targeted medication administration and the mitigation of illnesses associated with oxidative stress. Furthermore, the utilization of gelatine, a substance that is both biocompatible and biodegradable, is in accordance with the increasing need for nanomaterials that are environmentally sustainable and safe for medicinal purposes. In its entirety, our study constitutes an innovative addition to the domain of CDs synthesis, presenting encouraging prospects for future investigations and advancements in the realms of medication administration and antioxidant treatment.

The CDs possess a crucial characteristic of being highly stable under diverse environmental circumstances. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains constant for a period exceeding 9 months when stored in a dark environment at a temperature of 4 °C, and for 3 months when stored at room temperature (Fig. 3a). We investigated the impact of ionic strength on the stability of CDs by varying the concentration of NaCl in a 10 mM PBS (pH = 7.4) solution. The fluorescence signal exhibited minimal variation across a broad range (0-2 M) of NaCl concentrations, as indicated in Fig. 3b. Furthermore, the degree of photo-bleaching was also conducted in the presence of white light continuously for 60 min. For the purpose of making a relative comparison, we selected Rhodamine B as a representative synthetic dye commonly employed in cell culture investigations. Based on the observations from Fig. 3(c,d), it can be concluded that there were no significant progressive declines in UV–vis and fluorescence intensity. Once again, it demonstrates the superior durability of CDs compared to other synthetic dyes now on the market.

Evaluating the stability of nanoparticle size is a critical aspect in colloidal systems. This work entails a comprehensive evaluation of the stability of PACDs in an aqueous dispersion. The dimensions of the PACDs have been determined using the plot produced from the DLS experiment, as shown in Fig. 4 (a–d). Initially, the DLS results validated that the mean dimensions of the PACDs were around 2.5 nm. The systematic quantification of dimensions also carries additional significance in relation to stability. Furthermore, the DLS study was evaluated over a period of 3 weeks, resulting in little changes in the diameter of the PACDs. This implies that the artificially produced PACDs exhibit remarkable durability when exposed to water, suggesting a prolonged lifespan for the PACDs. In order to improve understanding of size stability, we also carried out a comparable experiment utilizing foetal bovine serum (FBS) as the dispersing agent for the PACDs. The installation was important since cell culture and other research conducted in vitro or in vivo frequently employ FBS medium. Observations in FBS medium indicated that the average size of the PACDs was approximately equivalent to that in water/PBS media. Nevertheless, the hydrodynamic diameter of PACDs exhibited negligible growth when maintained in FBS media for an extended duration, in contrast to their growth in PBS or water. It can be hypothesised that the primary attachment of extended FBS chains to the surface cannot be ruled out, given its supramolecular characteristics. The DLS data revealed a marginal augmentation in the dimensions of PACDs, which had an inconsequential effect on their quality.

This study examined the antioxidant characteristics of PACDs by utilizing DPPH and ABTS radicals. The DPPH free radical (FR) is frequently used to assess the antioxidant capacity of nanoparticles. The antioxidant capabilities of PACDs were evaluated using the DPPH free radical scavenging experiment. The assessment of antioxidant activity often involves the use of a DPPH radical, which reacts with an antioxidant and produces spectrophotometric changes. This method is efficient, straightforward, and cost-effective. DPPH is a stable free radical due to its composition of nitrogen atoms containing lone-pair electrons and being surrounded by three benzene rings. When DPPH interacts with an antioxidant, it undergoes a reaction in which it absorbs a hydrogen radical. This reaction results in the formation of a complex called DPPH-H and causes the compound to change colour from a deep violet to a pale yellow [53]. The reduction of the DPPH radical can be evaluated by measuring the absorbance at 517 nm compared to a blank sample. The DPPH radical-scavenging capacity of PACDs was evaluated at various concentrations, ranging from 0 to 800 μg/mL (Fig. 5a). The absorbance value exhibited a progressive decline as the amount of the CDs recahed to 600 μg/mL. Further inclusion of PACDs results in a small change in antioxidant activity, eventually reaching a point of saturation at approximately 800 μg/mL. By observing the transition from blue-green to colorless, it was discovered that PACDs can scavenge ABTS• + in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 5b). The validity was further confirmed by a decline in absorbance when the concentration of PACDs increased gradually from 0 to 800 μg/mL. The scavenging effects of ABTS• + showed a direct correlation with the dosage, reaching saturation at a concentration of 400 μg/mL of PACDs. The primary elements contributing to the increased antioxidant activity in the ABTS method are the solubility and effective distribution of PACDs in a water-based environment. The hydrogen transfer from the hydroxyl groups of PACDs can efficiently reduce and extinguish the free radicals ABTS• and DPPH•. For a better understanding of the interaction, the radical scavenging process used by CDs is demonstrated. Surface nitrogen and oxygen functions are believed to be involved in the scavenging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [54,55]. This phenomenon can be attributed to the covalent radius and valence states. A relationship has been found between the sp2 carbon domains, electron-donating doped sites, and primary radical scavenging sites. These sites are crucial in free radical annihilation through adduct formation and spin delocalization across the conjugated carbon backbone [54,56]. Dopants can improve the process of radical scavenging by donating e− to radicals. This is followed by either a transfer of protons or the formation of an adduct [41,57]. The ROS scavenging activity of CDs may be connected to the H-donor behaviour of specific functional groups, like -OH, -COOH, and -NH2, located at the edge-site. These groups and the free electron delocalization and stabilization within the carbon network may contribute to this activity. Fig. 5c illustrates the overall concept of the radical scavenging activity of PACDs [56].

Currently, chemotherapy is the main approach for treating cancer, and DOX is frequently recommended as a chemotherapy drug. The effectiveness of chemotherapy in advanced cancer was limited by its substantial toxicities. Considerable resources have been allocated to studying the delivery mechanism and improving the accurate administration of DOX in order to reduce its negative impacts. The paper states that the pH level in the tumour tissue falls between the ranges of 6.5 to 7.2, indicating a somewhat acidic environment. Nevertheless, the lysosomes present in the cancer cells have a higher level of acidity. The acidic environment acts as an ideal trigger for the controlled release of medications. Multiple researchers have developed a variety of drug-loading devices that respond to changes in pH, allowing for regulated delivery of pharmaceuticals. The small intestine is widely acknowledged to maintain a neutral pH and acts as the main location for nutrition absorption. Normally, food stays in the stomach for approximately 2–3 h before it is absorbed into the small intestine. We performed a study to examine the release behaviours of PACDs-DOX in various pH settings, namely in PBS buffer, SGF, and SIF. The pH-dependency of PACDs-DOX (Fig. 6 ) was seen in contrast to DOX. The release of PACDs-DOX after 72 h was 63 %, 22 %, and 11.4 % at pH levels of 5.1, 6.8, and 7.4, respectively. The instability of the –C=N– link in PACDs-DOX may be the reason for its susceptibility to dissociation in an acidic environment. PACDs-DOX exhibited higher release capacity in simulated gastric fluid (SGF) at pH 1.3 compared to plasma at pH 7.4 and simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) at pH 7.5. This can be ascribed to the distinctly acidic pH of SGF.

The release of PACDs-DOX on the tumour surface was reproduced in a PBS solution (pH = 5.0) in vitro. The confirmation of drug release has been established based on their UV spectra, as depicted in Fig. 7a. The release data was analysed using two mathematical models, specifically the Weibull model and the Higuchi model. The Higuchi and Weibull models are of great significance in the field of drug delivery due to their provision of mathematical frameworks that are crucial for comprehending, forecasting, and enhancing the kinetics of drug release from different dose forms. The aforementioned models function as predictive instruments, facilitating the development of drug delivery systems that possess precise release profiles that are essential for achieving therapeutic effectiveness. Through the clarification of fundamental release mechanisms, such as Fickian diffusion or erosion-controlled release, researchers are able to provide guidance for the optimization of formulations. This, in turn, allows for the precise adjustment of factors such as polymer composition and drug loading. Furthermore, these models play a crucial role in ensuring regulatory compliance by providing uniform approaches to characterize the behaviour of drug release, which is vital for the approval procedures. The utility of these tools extends to the field of comparative analysis, enabling researchers to quantitatively evaluate and compare various formulations. This ultimately contributes to the advancement of pharmaceutical products that are both safer and more effective.

The Weibull model is a mathematical representation that characterises the increase in medication release over time. The model is expressed by the subsequent equation:
(1)
F
t
= 1 − exp
−
t λ
β
F(t), λ, and β represent the cumulative drug release at time t, the scale parameter, and the shape parameter, respectively. When applying the Weibull model to analyse drug release kinetics, it is crucial to consider the drug delivery system's characteristics, the formulation properties, and the experimental circumstances. Scientists frequently do experiments to measure the amount of pharmaceuticals released at different time intervals, and then use regression analysis to fit the data to the equation of the Weibull model.

The Higuchi model is a commonly used mathematical model to describe the release of pharmaceuticals from pharmaceutical dosage forms, particularly when diffusion is the primary mechanism governing drug release. This hypothesis is based on Fick's law of diffusion and suggests that drug release occurs through the process of drug disintegration.

The Higuchi model is defined by the following equation:
(2)
Q
t
= k .
t
1 2
Q(t) is the cumulative drug release at time t, while k is the Higuchi constant that represents the rate of drug release. In the Higuchi model, the square root of time corresponds to drug release that is directly proportional to the square root of time. This feature signifies diffusion-controlled release mechanisms.

The technique of plotting the cumulative drug release (Q(t)) versus the square root of time is widely adopted when fitting experimental data to the Higuchi model. If the drug release follows a diffusion-controlled mechanism, the graph should display a linear connection. Fig. 7b and c illustrate the cumulative release profile of the drug compounds at varying pH levels and different simulated physiological fluids, respectively. The gradient of the linear segment of the graph is subsequently utilised to compute the Higuchi constant, which imparts insights on the rate at which the drug is released. From the data shown in Fig. 7d, it is clear that the fitting coefficient (R2) for the Weibull model had the highest value (R2 = 0.994) compared to the Higuchi model. It can be inferred that the release of the medication PACDs-DOX is likely due to the Weibull model. Furthermore, the shape parameter in the Weibull model exerts a substantial influence on the process by which drug molecules are released. In theory, there are three factors to consider in this context. When β < 1, the release pattern is classified as an initial burst release, which follows the normal kinetics of a first-order reaction. If β = 1, the release follows a zero-order pattern. For β > 1, the release exhibits a persistent sigmoidal behaviour. In this scenario, all the data regarding the release fits indicate a somewhat higher value than 1, suggesting time-dependent sustained release behaviour. More precisely, the data obtained at a lower pH demonstrates superior control compared to the data obtained in a higher pH environment. Performing in vitro drug release studies can help predict the effectiveness of medications when administered in vivo. The Higuchi model demonstrates the fitted data, as depicted in Fig. 7e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytocompatibility assessment of the PACDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 displays the results of an in vitro experiment on the growth of human epithelial corneal cells. The study investigated the impact of PACDs nanoparticles and a tissue culture plate (TCP) on cell proliferation at 24 and 72 h. The cell viability was evaluated by quantifying the level of cell mortality in the presence of the sample. The vitality of cells is directly proportional to their metabolic activity. The MTT assay indicated negligible cellular mortality. The quantity of cell deaths exhibited a direct correlation with the concentration of PACDs, as depicted in Fig. 8. The degree of cellular demise exhibited minimal fluctuations. After 24 and 72 h, the cell morphology remained unchanged, demonstrating favourable cell proliferation and attachment in the presence of nanoparticles. The findings of this investigation indicate that fibroblast cell lines do not demonstrate any cytotoxic effects.

This study introduces a direct, effective, and rapid one-step method for producing N-doped PACDs. A comprehensive investigation was conducted to assess the antioxidant and radical scavenging capabilities of PACDs, utilizing both the DPPH and ABTS methodologies. The PACDs demonstrated remarkable antioxidant activity in both methodologies, above 80 %. In addition, PACDs were employed as a nano-vector for the purpose of studying the release of the model drug DOX. The release characteristics of the doped PACDs were shown to be dependent on pH, with a higher degree of regulation observed at pH 7.4 in comparison to pH 5.1 and 6.8. The in vitro assessment of the cytotoxicity of the PACDs revealed negligible cell death, and the cellular composition remained unchanged after 24 and 72 h. This finding suggests that nanoparticles possess the capacity to augment cellular proliferation and adherence. The potential for developing a highly advanced and versatile substance lies in the fabrication of a biocompatible luminous nanodot that can be activated to release medications and function as an antioxidant nanomaterial.

Afzal Hussain: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohamed Fahad Alajmi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. S. Ganguly: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author thanks the generous support from the researchers supporting project number (RSPD2024R980) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications
ACS Cent. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, J., R. Li, and B. Yang, Carbon dots: A new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications. ACS Central Science, 2020. 6(12): p. 2179-2195.
